title: Coordinate Systems
subtitle: How robots navigate space
description: Learn how robots use X and Y coordinates to move precisely, and how coordinate systems are the foundation of all robot motion.

intuitive.hook: How does the robot know where to go on the paper?
intuitive.tutor_intro: Have you ever played a game where you had to find something on a map using letters and numbers, like A3 or C7? The robot uses the same idea — it has an X direction (left and right) and a Y direction (up and down). Every spot on the paper has its own unique address!
intuitive.starter_prompt: draw a plus sign in the center of the paper showing X and Y directions

structural.tutor_intro: Now let's use coordinate blocks. You can tell the robot exactly where to go using numbers. Try: move_to(100, 50) — that's 100mm to the right and 50mm up from the starting corner.
structural.starter_prompt: use move_to blocks to draw a square with corners at (50,50), (150,50), (150,150), (50,150)

precise.code_scaffold:
# SketchBot coordinate systems
import sketchbot

# Home position is (0, 0) — bottom-left of canvas
# X increases to the right, Y increases upward
# Canvas is 297mm x 210mm (A4)

sketchbot.move_to(148.5, 105)  # Move to center
sketchbot.draw_path([
    (50, 50),
    (247, 50),
    (247, 160),
    (50, 160),
    (50, 50)
])
precise.tutor_intro: In code, every position is a 2-tuple (x, y). The robot's workspace is a 2D Euclidean plane. You can use numpy arrays for paths, trigonometric functions for curves, and linear algebra for transformations.
precise.starter_prompt: import numpy as np; draw a grid of 5x5 dots evenly spaced across the canvas

title: Path Planning
subtitle: Bezier curves and smooth motion
description: Explore how robots plan their paths — the difference between jagged and smooth motion, and how Bezier curves work.

intuitive.hook: Why does the robot draw smooth curves instead of jagged lines?
intuitive.tutor_intro: Have you ever seen a race car take a turn? It doesn't jerk — it glides. The robot uses something called a Bezier curve to move smoothly between points. Let me show you the difference between a sharp zigzag path and a smooth curved path.
intuitive.starter_prompt: draw a smooth wave from the left side of the paper to the right

structural.tutor_intro: Drag the control points to shape your curve! The two small points control the curvature — pull them closer together for a sharper turn, further apart for a gentler curve. This is exactly how Bezier curves work.
structural.starter_prompt: use the waypoint editor to draw a smooth S-curve across the canvas

precise.code_scaffold:
import numpy as np
import sketchbot

def bezier(p0, p1, p2, p3, n=100):
    """Cubic Bezier curve through 4 control points."""
    t = np.linspace(0, 1, n)
    x = (1-t)**3*p0[0] + 3*(1-t)**2*t*p1[0] + 3*(1-t)*t**2*p2[0] + t**3*p3[0]
    y = (1-t)**3*p0[1] + 3*(1-t)**2*t*p1[1] + 3*(1-t)*t**2*p2[1] + t**3*p3[1]
    return list(zip(x, y))

path = bezier((20,105), (80,20), (200,190), (270,105))
sketchbot.draw_path(path)
precise.tutor_intro: A cubic Bezier curve is defined by 4 control points. P₀ and P₃ are the endpoints; P₁ and P₂ are 'handles' that pull the curve toward them without actually touching them.
precise.starter_prompt: write a bezier function and use it to draw a figure-8 (infinity symbol)

title: Geometry Through Drawing
subtitle: Shapes, symmetry & patterns
description: Discover how geometry comes alive when a robot draws it. Triangles, circles, fractals, and symmetry all have real mathematical structure.

intuitive.hook: How many straight lines does it take to make a circle?
intuitive.tutor_intro: Here's a cool trick: a circle is just a polygon with SO many sides that you can't see the corners anymore. Watch what happens when I draw a shape with 3 sides... then 6... then 60!
intuitive.starter_prompt: draw a regular polygon with 8 sides (an octagon)

structural.tutor_intro: Use the shape builder! Set the number of sides, size, and rotation. Try changing the number of sides from 3 to 100 — you'll literally watch a triangle become a circle.
structural.starter_prompt: use shape_builder to draw a 12-sided regular polygon, then a 5-pointed star

precise.code_scaffold:
import numpy as np
import sketchbot

def regular_polygon(cx, cy, r, n_sides, rotation_deg=0):
    """Draw a regular polygon centered at (cx, cy)."""
    angles = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, n_sides+1) + np.radians(rotation_deg)
    x = cx + r * np.cos(angles)
    y = cy + r * np.sin(angles)
    return list(zip(x, y))

# Draw nested polygons
for n in [3, 5, 8, 13, 21]:
    path = regular_polygon(148.5, 105, radius=n*5, n_sides=n)
    sketchbot.draw_path(path)
precise.math_notation: Pₙ(k) = (cx + r·cos(2πk/n + θ), cy + r·sin(2πk/n + θ)), k = 0..n
precise.tutor_intro: Regular polygons are defined by n equally spaced points on a circle. The interior angle sum is (n−2)·180°. As n→∞, the polygon converges to a circle.
precise.starter_prompt: draw the first 4 iterations of a Sierpinski triangle using recursion

title: Computer Vision
subtitle: How the robot sees the world
description: Understand how the robot uses a camera and AprilTag markers to locate itself in space — the same technology used in AR and autonomous vehicles.

intuitive.hook: How does the robot know where the paper is?
intuitive.tutor_intro: The robot can't feel the paper, but it CAN see it! It looks for special square markers called AprilTags at the corners. Each one has a unique pattern, like a tiny barcode. Once it sees all four, it knows exactly where the paper is and how it's tilted.
intuitive.starter_prompt: point the camera at the paper with AprilTag markers and describe what you see happening on screen

structural.tutor_intro: Look at the vision overlay! Each AprilTag gets highlighted when detected. You can see the corner points the robot found and the outline it computed for the paper. Try tilting the paper — watch how the detected outline changes in real time.
structural.starter_prompt: tilt the paper at different angles and observe how the robot's perspective changes

precise.code_scaffold:
import cv2
import numpy as np

# This is what the robot runtime does internally:
# 1. Detect AprilTag corners in image coordinates
# 2. Map them to known paper coordinates (world space)
# 3. Compute homography matrix H

src_pts = np.float32([[0,0],[297,0],[297,210],[0,210]])  # paper corners (mm)
dst_pts = np.float32(detected_tag_corners)               # pixel positions

H, _ = cv2.findHomography(src_pts, dst_pts)
# H transforms paper-space coordinates to image-space pixels
# This lets us overlay the drawing preview perfectly on the live image
precise.math_notation: p_img = H · p_world,  H ∈ ℝ³ˣ³ (homography matrix)
precise.tutor_intro: Computer vision uses a homography matrix H to map between two coordinate systems. Given 4 point correspondences (AprilTag corners), we can solve for the 8 degrees of freedom in H using Direct Linear Transform (DLT).
precise.starter_prompt: write a function that uses the homography matrix to check if a given paper coordinate is inside the detected canvas boundary

title: Control Theory
subtitle: Feedback loops and precision
description: Discover how robots correct their own errors using feedback loops — the same math that controls thermostats, cruise control, and rockets.

intuitive.hook: How does the robot draw a straight line without wobbling?
intuitive.tutor_intro: Imagine trying to walk in a perfectly straight line with your eyes closed. Hard right? The robot faces the same challenge — its motors aren't perfect. So it constantly checks where it is vs where it should be, and corrects itself. I'll show you what happens when the correction is too aggressive... and when it's just right.
intuitive.starter_prompt: draw ten parallel horizontal lines and see how straight the robot can make them

structural.tutor_intro: Use the PID sliders! There are three knobs: P (how aggressively it corrects), I (how much it learns from past errors), and D (how fast it's correcting). Find the settings that make the robot draw the smoothest line.
structural.starter_prompt: tune the P and D sliders to minimize overshoot on a straight 100mm line

precise.code_scaffold:
class PIDController:
    def __init__(self, kp, ki, kd):
        self.kp, self.ki, self.kd = kp, ki, kd
        self.integral = 0
        self.prev_error = 0
    
    def update(self, setpoint, measurement, dt):
        error = setpoint - measurement
        self.integral += error * dt
        derivative = (error - self.prev_error) / dt
        self.prev_error = error
        return self.kp*error + self.ki*self.integral + self.kd*derivative

# Tune these for the robot:
pid = PIDController(kp=2.0, ki=0.5, kd=0.1)
precise.tutor_intro: PID control is one of the most important algorithms in engineering. P corrects proportionally to current error, I eliminates steady-state error by integrating history, D dampens oscillation using the derivative.
precise.starter_prompt: simulate a PID controller in Python and plot the step response for three different Kp values

title: Systems Thinking
subtitle: Design your own challenge
description: Learn to think like an engineer by designing, building, and testing your own robot challenge from scratch.

intuitive.hook: What would you want a robot to draw for your school?
intuitive.tutor_intro: Every great invention starts with a question: 'What problem am I trying to solve?' Engineers call this 'systems thinking' — understanding all the parts and how they fit together. Today you're going to design something! What would you want the robot to draw, and why?
intuitive.starter_prompt: design a drawing that would teach younger students about something you care about

structural.tutor_intro: Use the challenge builder to design a step-by-step learning activity. You'll define: the goal, the steps, hints for students, and how to know when it's done. This is exactly how the challenges in SketchBot were built!
structural.starter_prompt: build a 3-step challenge that teaches another student one thing about your favorite subject

precise.tutor_intro: Systems engineering formalizes design as: specify requirements → design architecture → implement → test → iterate. Write a design document for your challenge, including measurable success criteria and failure modes.
precise.starter_prompt: write a complete design document for a robot challenge that teaches a concept from your own curriculum

title: Trigonometry in Motion
subtitle: Sine, cosine, and circular paths
description: See how sin and cos are more than just test problems — they are the actual math that makes the robot draw circles, waves, and spirals.

intuitive.hook: Did you know a circle is made of two waves?
intuitive.tutor_intro: Here's something wild: a circle is secretly made from two waves — sine and cosine — working together. The robot will draw a sine wave for you now. Watch the height of the line go up and down, up and down... exactly like a wave in the ocean.
intuitive.starter_prompt: draw a wave pattern that repeats 3 times across the paper

structural.tutor_intro: Try the wave builder! You can control: amplitude (how tall), frequency (how many waves fit), and phase (where it starts). Notice how changing frequency changes the number of peaks. This is the math of sound, light, and even music!
structural.starter_prompt: use wave_builder to draw two overlapping waves with different frequencies — this is called a beat pattern

precise.code_scaffold:
import numpy as np
import sketchbot

# Parametric equations for curves
t = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 300)

# Circle: x=cos(t), y=sin(t)
circle = list(zip(148.5 + 80*np.cos(t), 105 + 80*np.sin(t)))

# Lissajous figure: change a and b
a, b = 3, 2
lissajous = list(zip(148.5 + 80*np.sin(a*t), 105 + 80*np.sin(b*t + np.pi/4)))

sketchbot.draw_path(lissajous)
precise.tutor_intro: Parametric equations express curves as functions of a parameter t. Lissajous figures emerge when a/b is rational — and they're used in oscilloscopes to measure phase relationships. Change a and b to see different patterns.
precise.starter_prompt: draw a rose curve: r = cos(k·θ) in polar coordinates. Try k=3, k=5, k=7 and observe the pattern.

title: Cone Ring Gauntlet
subtitle: Ultrasonic aim, line sensors for balance
description: Design a robot that hunts traffic cones inside a ring using an ultrasonic rangefinder to line up pushes, while line IR sensors keep you from driving off the edge — without tipping over. Tutorial flow: (1) calibrate line thresholds on your mat, (2) sweep to acquire cones in range, (3) cap motor power and test edge recovery.

intuitive.hook: Can your robot "see" a cone it cannot photograph?
intuitive.tutor_intro: Welcome to the Cone Ring! Picture a low arena with a bold black border. Ultrasonic sensors send out tiny sound clicks and listen for the echo — that gives distance to the nearest cone. Line IR sensors stare at the floor: dark line means "edge is close"; bright means "safe." Your job is to choreograph those two senses so the bot shoves cones without driving off the cliff or ramming so hard it flips. Mini-tutorial: first stand still and log raw line values on white vs black; then spin slowly and watch distance spikes when a cone enters the beam; only then chain forward motion.
intuitive.starter_prompt: write a 4-step test plan: calibrate IR, log ultrasonic vs cone position, one slow push trial, then add edge escape — note one failure mode for each step

structural.tutor_intro: Stack blocks in a sense → decide → act loop. Wire ultrasonic distance into a threshold ("cone near enough to push"). Pair left/right line IR so if one side sees black first, you curve away from the ring edge. Add a max motor power cap so momentum stays gentle — fewer tip-overs. Add a timer so "hunt" never runs forever: every 2.5s without a valid distance, nudge rotate 15° and try again.
structural.starter_prompt: build blocks for: HUNT (rotate until distance in 15–60 cm), ALIGN (differential drive to center the echo), PUSH (capped power), EDGE (both lines or either line triggers reverse+turn) — label which block runs fastest in your loop

precise.code_scaffold:
# Cone ring — ultrasonic + line IR fusion (pseudocode for a differential drive)
ULTRA_NEAR_CM = 18
LINE_DARK = 0.35  # normalized reflectance threshold
MAX_PWM = 0.55

def read_ultra_cm() -> float: ...
def line_left() -> float: ...
def line_right() -> float: ...
def drive(L: float, R: float): ...

def cone_ring_tick():
    d = read_ultra_cm()
    ll, lr = line_left(), line_right()
    if min(ll, lr) < LINE_DARK:
        drive(-MAX_PWM * 0.6, -MAX_PWM * 0.6)  # back off
        turn_away_from_edge(ll, lr)
        return
    if d < ULTRA_NEAR_CM:
        drive(MAX_PWM * 0.45, MAX_PWM * 0.45)  # gentle shove
    else:
        hunt_scan()  # rotate or sweep to acquire cones
precise.tutor_intro: Ultrasonic ranging assumes the first echo is the cone — watch for ring walls creating ghost readings. IR line sensors respond to reflectance; calibrate thresholds on your actual mat. Fuse slow: low-pass filter distance, debounce line hits, and limit jerk (Δv/Δt) so the center of mass stays inside the support polygon. Tutorial extension: if two echoes compete, prefer the reading that matches last known cone bearing; reject jumps larger than your max closing speed × Δt.
precise.starter_prompt: implement median-of-3 on distance, 50 ms debounce on line trip, and a jerk cap — document the numeric values you chose and why

title: Sumo Arena
subtitle: Push the rival bot out first
description: Two robots, one ring: last machine inside wins. Strategy, torque, timing, and sensors turn a shove into a sport. Tutorial flow: (1) prove edge sensors stop you at the border every time, (2) add opponent seek, (3) tune charge vs escape timing.

intuitive.hook: Is sumo about strength — or about knowing where the edge is?
intuitive.tutor_intro: Mini sumo is chess at 500 RPM. You want enough grip to shove, but not so much spin that you fly out backwards. Most bots read the white ring border with sensors under the nose; many hunt opponents with short-range IR or ultrasonic. Picture your robot as a bulldozer with reflexes: if the edge flickers, you backpedal before a wheel crosses. Try this lesson arc: shadow practice (motors off, drag bot to feel the ring), then edge-only autonomous, then add seek.
intuitive.starter_prompt: for each phase — shadow, edge-only, seek+charge — write one success metric you can observe in under 60 seconds

structural.tutor_intro: Model the match as states: SEEK (scan for opponent), CHARGE (full forward when target in arc), EDGE_ESCAPE (reverse + curve when border detected), COOLDOWN (short pause after a big hit). Swap priorities so edge safety always preempts attack. Sketch a ladder diagram: SEEK at the bottom priority, EDGE_ESCAPE at the top — anything below must yield when the ladder fires.
structural.starter_prompt: use blocks so COOLDOWN can only run after EDGE_ESCAPE or a collision timer, never after a clean SEEK — explain why that prevents stall-outs

precise.math_notation: τ_wheel ∝ μ·N — traction limits push; prioritize CoM low inside the ring polygon
precise.tutor_intro: Sumo is dominated by traction and center of mass. The control problem is hybrid: continuous drive laws inside each state, discrete jumps on sensor events. Consider opponent acquisition rate vs edge sampling rate — the faster loop should own safety. Add hysteresis on border readings so a single noisy sample cannot cancel CHARGE; require two consecutive edge hits or a short persistence window.
precise.starter_prompt: code edge detection with a 30–50 ms persistence filter and log state transitions during a mock match — count spurious escapes

title: Maze Marathon
subtitle: Wall-follow, nodes, and memory
description: From left-hand rule to stepping through a grid: learn how maze-solving robots turn corridors into a solvable graph. Tutorial flow: (1) reliable wall detection, (2) consistent intersection turns, (3) log a path string, (4) optimize on run two.

intuitive.hook: If you always hug the left wall, do you always escape?
intuitive.tutor_intro: A maze-solving bot feels the world with bump sensors, short IR, or a time-of-flight sweep. The classic trick beginners love is wall following — pick a wall and never let go. It does not work in every maze, but it is a beautiful introduction to persistent rules creating emergent paths. Watch how corners become "nodes" where you choose forward, left, or right. Practice drill: drive three perfect 90° turns in an empty box — if your turns drift, fix that before trusting any maze rule.
intuitive.starter_prompt: draw a tiny 3x3 maze on paper, run left-hand rule by hand, and mark where you revisit the same cell — what does that tell you about loops?

structural.tutor_intro: Represent the maze as intersections. Each block step is: advance to the next node, read openings, enqueue decisions. Build a simple stack or string of turns (like LFR letters) to remember a discovered path — that is the bridge from wall-follow to systematic search. Add a "segment" block: drive until the next opening or wall contact, so your graph edges have consistent length.
structural.starter_prompt: encode LFRBLF for one solved maze, then apply the standard simplification rules (replace LBL with S, etc.) on paper before you code

precise.code_scaffold:
# Tremaux / simplified exploration — sketch for discussion
from enum import Enum
class Dir(Enum): N = 0; E = 1; S = 2; W = 3

def left_hand_step(walls_open, facing):
    # walls_open: [front, left, right] relative to robot
    if not walls_open[1]:  # left open
        return 'turn_left', (facing - 1) % 4
    if not walls_open[0]:
        return 'forward', facing
    if not walls_open[2]:
        return 'turn_right', (facing + 1) % 4
    return 'uturn', (facing + 2) % 4

# Flood-fill on a known grid is O(n) after mapping — students often implement BFS on cell grid
precise.math_notation: Maze as graph G=(V,E); shortest path ⇒ BFS on V if map known
precise.tutor_intro: Wall-following is a policy, not a guarantee of optimality. For competition mazes, teams often map cells with encoders + gyros, then flood-fill from the goal. Discuss error: wheel slip makes odometry drift — periodic landmark fixes matter. When you have a full map, BFS gives shortest path in unweighted grids; compare that path to your logged LFR string length.
precise.starter_prompt: simulate a 5x5 grid maze in Python: run BFS from start to goal, then animate left-hand rule on the same grid and print total steps — note when they diverge badly
